open_questions.question: Connection to Sir John Standley, officer in Cromwell's army
open_questions.notes: EH notes Sir John Standley was an officer in Cromwell's army and that the family was then an old one with no known connection to the famous English Stanley family. EH argues the Connecticut settlers spelled the name correctly as 'Standley' and the later standardization to 'Stanley' was a mistake. Research at Farmington Historical Society and English army records may confirm.
open_questions.action: Check Cromwell's army officer records for Sir John Standley; check Farmington Historical Society; compare with Stanley family pedigrees

sources.full: Israel P. Warren D.D., The Stanley Families of America as Descended from John, Timothy, and Thomas Stanley of Hartford, CT, 1636 (1887)

parents.father_research_notes: John Stanley, emigrant from England — settled Hartford 1636

political_offices.title: Deputy to General Court
political_offices.notes: Warren: 'almost continually for thirty-seven years — with few exceptions, the longest term of service known in the annals of the state'

notable.notable_blurb: Long-serving Farmington deputy and militia captain

narrative_blocks.header: He marched against the Pequots at age thirteen
narrative_blocks.body: John Stanley came to Hartford as a boy in his uncle's care. In 1637, only thirteen, he joined the colony's expedition against the Pequots, the year after the settlers arrived.

narrative_blocks.header: He served thirty-seven years as a deputy
narrative_blocks.body: From 1659 to 1696 Stanley represented Farmington at the General Court almost without a break, one of the longest terms of service in the colony's records.

narrative_blocks.header: He built the house that became Farmington's museum
narrative_blocks.body: Around 1665 Stanley built a home on the Farmington street for his second wife. Long known as the Stanley-Whitman House, it survives today as the town's museum.

narrative_blocks.header: He rose to captain in King Philip's War
narrative_blocks.body: During King Philip's War Stanley served as a lieutenant and then captain, the rank that gave him the name by which he was always known, Capt. John Stanley.

narrative_blocks.header: He married the same day as his sister
narrative_blocks.body: On December 5, 1645, John Stanley married Sarah Scott at Hartford. The same day his sixteen-year-old sister Ruth married Isaac Moore, and both young couples settled together at Farmington.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: He acquired the property where this house was later built.

military_service.notes: Warren: 'when a mere boy of thirteen, he went in the expedition against the Pequots, the very year after the settlers came to Hartford'
Lieutenant then Captain; received 120-acre land grant from General Court 1674 and another 1687

bio.title: Captain
bio.notes: Surname also spelled 'Stanley' — EH argues 'Standley' is the correct original spelling
